name: Camino Primitivo: Tineo and Alto de Lavadoira Loop
name_original: Alto de Lavadoira desde Tineo — circular por el Camino Primitivo
description: Cycle the Alto de Lavadoira from Tineo, a difficult 30.2-mile circular route on the Camino Primitivo with 3293 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Tineo, a municipality in Asturias, Spain, offers diverse landscapes characterized by significant elevation gains and varied terrain. The region features a blend of challenging routes through river valleys like the Río Narcea and Esva River Gorges, alongside scenic paths that traverse hills and traditional villages. This area provides a rich natural heritage for touring cyclists, with mostly well-paved surfaces and some unpaved segments. Best touring cycling routes around Tineo The most popular touring cycling route is Route of Los Hospitales including Alto de La Marta and Pola de Allande — circular from Campiello, a 26.7 miles (42.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 35 minutes to complete. This difficult route features the Alto de La Marta and offers a challenging circular journey. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Santuario del Acebo Loop, a difficult 21.8 miles (35.2 km) path. This route includes significant elevation gain, leading to the Santuario del Acebo. Local touring cyclists also love the Cascada de Nonaya from Tineo — circular by the Río Narcea, a 36.7 miles (59.0 km) trail leading through the Río Narcea valley, often completed in about 4 hours 18 minutes. Touring cycling around Tineo is defined by river valleys, rolling hills, and the historic Camino Primitivo. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The routes in Tineo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 390 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Tineo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Tineo?
A: Tineo offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 45 options recorded on komoot. These routes cater to various preferences, though most are considered difficult due to the region's significant elevation changes.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Tineo?
A: The majority of touring cycling routes in Tineo are classified as difficult, with 40 out of 45 routes falling into this category. There are also a few moderate and easy options available, but cyclists should generally expect challenging terrain with considerable elevation gains.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Tineo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Tineo are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.6 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, the challenging climbs, and the scenic beauty of the Asturian countryside.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Tineo?
A: Yes, Tineo features several excellent circular touring cycling routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029983565/route-of-los-hospitales-including-alto-de-la-marta-and-pola-de-allande-circular-from-campiello" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of Los Hospitales including Alto de La Marta and Pola de Allande — circular from Campiello&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 42.9 km loop. Another favorite is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370629545/santuario-del-acebo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santuario del Acebo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers significant elevation gain over 35.2 km.

Q: Are there routes that follow the Camino de Santiago?
A: Yes, Tineo is an important stop on the historic Primitive Way (Camino Primitivo) to Santiago. While primarily a pilgrimage route for walkers, sections are also popular with touring cyclists seeking a cultural and scenic journey. You can explore routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370740656/camino-primitivo-tineo-and-alto-de-lavadoira-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Primitivo: Tineo and Alto de Lavadoira Loop&lt;/a&gt; to experience this historic path.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in Tineo?
A: Tineo's routes offer stunning natural scenery. You can encounter dramatic landscapes like the Esva River Gorges, or visit the Natural Monument of the Principality of Asturias, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2873224" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valentín's Oak&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also feature significant mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2873224" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de La Marta&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5145648" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Lavadoira&lt;/a&gt;, offering expansive views.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by waterfalls?
A: Yes, you can find routes that lead to beautiful natural attractions like waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370747317/cascada-de-nonaya-from-tineo-circular-by-the-rio-narcea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada de Nonaya from Tineo — circular by the Río Narcea&lt;/a&gt; is a notable route that takes you through the Río Narcea valley, offering a chance to see the Nonaya waterfall.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites along the routes?
A: Absolutely. Tineo is rich in cultural heritage. You might pass by the 13th-century Cistercian &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5594153" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of Santa María la Real de Obona&lt;/a&gt;, an emblematic point on the Camino Primitivo. The region also features the Sacred Art Museum and the restored Palace of Los García de Tineo, now the Municipal House of Culture, offering glimpses into the area's history.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Tineo?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Tineo is generally during the spring and autumn months. The weather is typically milder, and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summer can also be pleasant, but be prepared for potentially warmer temperatures and more tourists, especially on popular routes like the Camino Primitivo. Winters can be cold and wet, with some higher passes potentially experiencing snow.

Q: Is Tineo suitable for family touring cycling?
A: While Tineo's touring cycling routes are predominantly difficult with significant elevation, there are a few easier options available. Families with older children or those accustomed to challenging rides might enjoy some of the less strenuous sections or shorter loops. However, beginners and families with very young children might find the terrain demanding.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Tineo?
A: Many outdoor areas in Tineo are dog-friendly, but it's always best to check local regulations for specific routes or protected natural areas. Ensure your dog is well-behaved, on a leash where required, and that you carry waste bags. Some routes, especially those with challenging terrain or shared paths, might not be suitable for all dogs.

Q: Are there options for parking at the trailheads?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. In larger towns like Tineo or near popular starting points for routes, you can often find designated parking areas. For more remote trailheads, roadside parking might be an option, but always ensure you park safely and legally, without obstructing local access or traffic.

Q: How can I reach the touring cycling routes using public transport?
A: Public transport options in Tineo and the surrounding rural areas can be limited. Buses connect Tineo with larger towns in Asturias, but services to specific trailheads might be infrequent or non-existent. It's advisable to research local bus schedules in advance or consider using a car to access your chosen starting points.
tips: Tinéu or Tineo is a beautiful place to start or discover an adventure. The center is not that big but it has some interesting places to visit such as the Church of San Pedro, the building on Calle Mayor or simply taking in the views from the town.
Small chapel on the wayside of the Camino Primitivo
This is a well known local road port for local cyclists. If you are following the Camino Primitivo, you will be happy to know that from here you have a good descent towards Pola de Allande.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Alto de Lavadoira circular route for touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a "Hard bike ride" requiring "Very good fitness." It involves a substantial 1,000 meters of elevation gain over 48.6 kilometers, with relentless climbs and descents typical of the Camino Primitivo.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and challenging. You'll encounter both paved sections and unpaved dirt tracks and paths. Some segments can be rocky or muddy after rain, potentially requiring you to push your bike. Touring cyclists often use roads or alternative routes designed for bicycles to navigate the more difficult unpaved sections.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Alto de Lavadoira route?
faqs.answer: The route offers stunning Asturian scenery, including picturesque mountain views, lush forests, and traditional villages. Notable highlights include the Tineo Town Centre, the Hermitage of La Magdalena, and the challenging climb to Alto de Lavadoira itself. You might also pass historic sites like the Romanesque church and Monastery of Obona.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Alto de Lavadoira circular route from Tineo?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Tineo. While specific parking details aren't provided, you would typically look for parking within or near the Tineo Town Centre to begin your circular journey.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers spectacular scenery through the rugged Asturian mountains. While specific seasonal recommendations aren't detailed, generally, late spring to early autumn (May to October) would provide the most favorable weather conditions for cycling in Asturias, avoiding the harshest winter conditions and potential snow at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Alto de Lavadoira circular route or sections of the Camino Primitivo.

faqs.question: Is the Alto de Lavadoira circular route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as "difficult" and requires "Very good fitness." It is not recommended for beginners or families with young children due to its demanding terrain, significant elevation gain, and challenging unpaved sections.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this circular route from Tineo extensively uses sections of the Camino Primitivo. It also intersects with parts of "Etapa 4: Tinéu - Pola de Allande" and briefly touches upon "Etapa 3: Salas - Tinéu" and "Etapa 4b: Variante de Hospitales".

faqs.question: Is the Alto de Lavadoira circular route dog-friendly for cyclists?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions mentioned, given that this is a challenging touring bicycle route with varied and sometimes difficult terrain, it may not be ideal for cycling with dogs. Consider the physical demands on your dog and the safety aspects of navigating unpaved and potentially steep sections.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of the Camino Primitivo section of this route?
faqs.answer: The Camino Primitivo is known as the "Original Way" and holds the distinction of being the oldest Camino de Santiago route. It was first undertaken by King Alfonso II in the 9th century and offers a more serene and authentic pilgrimage experience with fewer crowds compared to other Camino routes.

faqs.question: Are there options to make the challenging Alto de Lavadoira route more accessible?
faqs.answer: Yes, for those seeking assistance with the demanding terrain and significant elevation gain, e-bikes are available for rent in the region. This can make the journey more accessible and enjoyable for cyclists who might find the route too strenuous on a traditional touring bicycle.
